data.title: We (Still) Don't Expect The Bank Of England To Hike Rates
data.description:
By James Smith, Developed Markets Economist, UK

No, the UK is not more vulnerable to the coming inflation shock

Financial markets are still applying the 2022 playbook to the Bank of England. The scale of repricing in interest rate expectations has been more dramatic than either the eurozone or the US. The implied UK rate one year from now has risen a full percentage point since the onset of war, and investors are still pricing close to two hikes by year-end.

That seems to imply that the UK is facing a more severe inflation shock than elsewhere. We disagree. That may have been true in 2022. And Britain may be a major energy importer. But its dependency is on natural gas. And fortunately, this is not yet a natural gas crisis. Wholesale prices are still a fraction of where they traded in the fallout of the Ukraine invasion.

Based on ING’s updated energy price forecasts, we think inflation is set to rise to 3% through May/June and bounce between 3.5-4% from July onwards. That’s virtually identical to our new eurozone inflation predictions. And if anything, the UK may be less exposed to the second-round inflationary effects that keep central bankers up at night.

The jobs market is weaker than in much of Europe, judging by vacancy data, thanks to the effects of last year’s tax rises. Those increases had minimal impact on inflation but a notable impact on hiring. It’s a reminder that firms have much less pricing power than they did in 2022. Likewise, workers don’t have nearly as much power to demand higher pay. Wage growth has already fallen sharply in recent months. Unemployment is set to rise further.

We're still not convinced the Bank will hike rates

Fiscal policy is also getting tighter. The structural deficit is still set to fall this year thanks to the freeze in income tax brackets. That’s in contrast to the post-pandemic period, but also Germany and its neighbouring economies, which are set to benefit from the wave of infrastructure spending coming online this year. Universal energy bill support looks unlikely.

The other major difference is that Bank of England interest rates are still widely seen as restrictive. The nominal neutral rate – a rate neither expansionary nor restrictive for the economy – is seen around 2.5-3%. Again, that’s a difference with the ECB, where its 2% deposit rate is largely seen as neutral. The centre of gravity is still down, if anything; remember the Bank of England was on the verge of cutting rates further before the war began.

Finally, the Bank of England itself has begun to strike a different tone to its continental neighbours. Governor Bailey recently made a rare and not-so-subtle intervention on market pricing, saying investors were “getting ahead of themselves” in pricing hikes.

We took this to mean an April rate hike is highly unlikely. We’re sticking with our call for the Bank to keep rates on hold this year. This marks a small divergence with the ECB, from which our team now expects a hike in June.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: Tesla Earnings Preview: Why The Dream Is Breaking Down
data.description:
Thesis

I'm not short on Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), and I don't need to be. All I need to do is avoid buying the stock. The market currently believes Tesla is worth approximately $391.95 per share, making its market capitalization roughly $1.43 trillion. However, the part of the business that creates revenue and produces cash is showing signs of slowdown; in 2025, vehicle deliveries declined to 1,636,129 vehicles, automotive revenue declined by nearly ten percent ($69.5 billion) and the group's operating margin remained flat at 4.6%. I think CEO Elon Musk has great vision, but I will never invest in anything based on market sentiment alone.

I believe Tesla is a good company, but right now at these prices, it is a bad investment. My entire thesis could end here, with this sentence. So I'll say it again: Tesla isn't a bad company, but at these prices, it's a bad investment. I wouldn't even bet on a dollar I received as a gift.

The company has admitted that it has transitioned from being “hardware centric” to becoming “a physical AI Company.” As well, in 2026 Tesla intends to increase growth through the addition of six new manufacturing lines covering vehicles, robotics, energy storage and battery technology. Unfortunately, this new direction will require large amounts of capital: according to Tesla's 10-K filing, in 2026 its CapEx will be over $20 billion and it acknowledges that such high CapEx periods may require additional financing above that generated solely from operations.

It is this phase that every Tesla investor needs to pay close attention to. At the same time that the core auto business is experiencing slow down, Tesla is significantly increasing both its spending and execution profile.

My first reaction when someone says “Tesla isn't just a car company,” will be “Fine,” but then ask them “What makes you think that?” Are they simply basing their thoughts on what they heard during a presentation, or are they looking at the actual financial data contained within the financial statements? Because if you examine the Income Statement and determine where the revenues come from, as of today the answer is far less romantic than many would like to portray it as, Tesla is still very much an automotive company, producing revenues primarily through automobile sales which represents approximately ninety percent of total revenue.

Ultimately, as an investor, I am going to base my conclusions on what I see in the numbers rather than what I hear in presentations or what the market anticipates happening in five years.

However, the market continues to price the stock as if there was no uncertainty about how things will play out in the future. Therefore, the point of this argument is not optimism, but it is the extreme optimism. In this analysis, I will mainly discuss the current market valuation, why it is a "not buyable" stock in my opinion, and I will talk about the upcoming quarterly results.

The data that changes everything

To me, there's one key metric that represents why I'm still very bearish on Tesla. That metric is deliveries. For those unaware, in the first quarter of 2026, the company produced 408,386 vehicles and delivered 358,023. To me, it doesn't seem like a small detail. Investors who own shares of Tesla should pay close attention to deliveries, because it indicates that in just three months, Tesla built over 50,000 more cars than it was able to sell. What does that mean for an auto manufacturer? If you build more cars than you can sell, then your inventory will start to grow, whether it sits in the yard waiting to be picked up by customers; in transit; ready to ship to dealerships; or already in inventory. Either way, demand is being outpaced by supply.

Analysts' consensus was 365,645 deliveries (Bloomberg consensus), while actual deliveries came in at 358,023. We aren't simply looking at a gap between the cars Tesla makes and sells. We are also seeing that the market had low expectations of Tesla's reported deliveries and even those expectations weren't met.

Item Model 3/Y Other Models Total Vehicles
Tesla consensus 351,179 14,466 365,645
Market estimate 351,704 17,200 368,903
Actual reported 341,893 16,130 358,023

UBS noted in their latest research note regarding Tesla, that based on their estimates, days of inventory grew to 35 from 21 in the prior quarter. That is the highest days of inventory level recorded since UBS began tracking this statistic.

While days of inventory can be useful when assessing operations and logistics, rising levels of days of inventory are a warning signal. As the days of inventory grows, it isn't simply a matter of timing. Inventory is growing faster than it historically has. I continue to believe that investors are underestimating this trend. Rising days of inventory levels indicate to me that the current operational issues surrounding car distribution are becoming more serious and indicative of larger problems surrounding final demand.

While I believe that this is not a one-time disconnect, rather a continuing disconnect between the volume of cars being manufactured and the volume of cars being demanded/sold. I see it as clear evidence that today the primary bottleneck facing Tesla is demand. At a time where Tesla's stock price continues to be seen as nearly limitless in terms of growth potential, this is an extremely relevant piece of information.

According to Reuters, the loss of the $7,500 US Federal Tax Credit has put additional pressure on demand. Additionally, Tesla is attempting to protect volumes through lower-priced versions of the Model 3 and Model Y. However, these lower prices come at the expense of further margin compression. In China, competition from BYD (BYDDF) and other domestic players continues to be intense. Tesla's share of the Chinese Electric Vehicle Market has decreased to 8% from 10% last year. Finally, in Europe, March registration numbers increased, however, I view this increase with great caution. These are monthly figures. Therefore, they do not eliminate a quarter worth of excess production. Nor do they address the root cause of lack of pricing power.

The Market Is Extrapolating the Wrong Quarter

Before I begin to discuss my personal expectations for the next quarterly results scheduled for April 22nd, I think it's appropriate to take a step back to the previous earnings release. On one hand, the fourth quarter of 2025 had an appearance of being solid; the company reported $24.9 billion in sales (revenue), a total gross margin of 20.1%, a non-credit automotive margin of 17.9%, free cash flow of $1.4 billion, and 1.1 million FSD (Full Self-Drive) subscribers. However, on the other hand, the market is taking the appearance of a good quarter and using it as a basis to raise the bar for the remainder of the year.

In my opinion, Q4 '25 was the highest point (or if you prefer, we can say it was the best moment of the ongoing cycle), not a feasible base for the future. In essence, many investors are looking at the best quarter and using that as their base to determine how the business will perform moving forward. I do not view it in that manner. The results were achieved during a period when they experienced a better mix, higher average price, and substantially better performance from the energy segment than they did at the start of 2026. It makes a big difference in how to interpret these numbers. When you establish your base off of a "clean" quarter, you run the risk of establishing unsustainable levels for earnings, margins, and cash generated from operations in subsequent periods.

That is exactly why I am interested in what happens during the next quarterly earnings call. I am not particularly concerned about whether Tesla reports earnings per share (EPS) slightly above consensus estimates, but rather whether the business will maintain growth in the coming quarters and whether Q4 2025 wasn't just a favorable outlier, and therefore a one-off. If volume declines, if inventory builds, and/or if the product mix begins to shift toward less expensive vehicles, then auto margins become less stable. Plainly stated: selling fewer cars at lower prices or providing greater incentives will mean that each car sold will produce lower net income. For a company such as Tesla that derives more than half of its total revenue from automobile sales, this continues to be the focal tenet to the investment thesis.

What really matters on April 22

I will focus on four specific metrics:

1. Auto Margin. While Tesla generated an impressive 17.9% automotive ex-credit non-GAAP margin in Q4 '25, I do not think we should assume that this was anything more than a temporary surge. Instead, I believe we need to determine what level of margin Tesla will generate going forward and above all, whether this will be a lasting and sustainable trend. I have my doubts about this, but I hope they're not just feelings.

2. Production Discipline. As demand slows down significantly, I want to know if Tesla will finally start producing vehicles at the same rate as demand is decreasing, or if they will continue to churn them out as though demand has not slowed down. The impact of this decision is significant, since inventory that cannot be delivered immediately ties up capital that could otherwise be used to generate cash flow.

3. Energy. Energy sales were 14.2Gwh in Q4, and only 8.8Gwh in Q1 ’26, substantially less than analysts had anticipated. According to UBS, the shortfall in energy sales alone could account for approximately $0.09 per share of earnings power for the quarter.

Clearly, this is not simply a volume issue, but it represents a problem of earnings quality since energy products generally represent high margin products. When these products are sold short of expectations, they weigh on the overall earnings performance of the company.

4. Implied Guidance for 2026. Perhaps even more importantly than any of the above items. I'm not so much interested in the narrative. I am interested in determining whether the numbers indicate that earnings risk remains firmly on the downside or not.

In my opinion, Tesla does not face the challenge that no one believes in its long-term prospects. On the contrary, the challenge is nearly the opposite – the stock prices in a very rosy picture of the future, while the present becomes increasingly complex. It takes little to turn such an expectation into disappointment.

A Cult Valuation, Not an Investment Valuation

The issue with valuations is where I believe many long-term investors in Tesla stopped thinking like investors and began to think like fans. The numbers from Seeking Alpha are very simple; Valuation Grade F. There is no single number or multiple, the entire table indicates the same thing. Tesla’s current P/E ratio is 193.37 times what analysts estimate will be earned in 2026. The next year, Tesla’s P/E ratio is projected to be 142.34 times estimated earnings for 2027. One final estimate places Tesla’s P/E ratio at 98.33 times estimated earnings for 2028.

Moving to the Enterprise Value (EV) side of things, Tesla currently trades at 14.09x forward EV/Sales, 95.60x forward EV/EBITDA and 252.78x forward EV/EBIT.

These are not demanding valuations. What these represent is a valuation that allows zero wiggle room and to me there can’t even be one weak quarter, there can’t be delays in executing plans, margins can’t begin to compress, nor can demand be lackluster. Anybody who buys Tesla stock today doesn’t buy a company. They purchase a perfect series of promises that all need to come true for years.

What is perhaps the most unbelievable aspect of this situation is how this valuation is being defended as if it was reasonable. It isn’t. If one is going to justify numbers such as those listed above, Tesla must be able to successfully perform the following 7 functions:

  1. increase auto volume;
  2. maintain pricing levels;
  3. protect margin levels;
  4. fund massive capital expenditures through some means;
  5. transition energy into a reliable revenue stream;
  6. generate significant amounts of revenue by leveraging software technology;
  7. and finally develop and deploy fully autonomous vehicles and humanoid robots.

The question is: when?

While these are the requirements needed to meet the standards established forth by Wall Street, the reality remains that the automotive industry represents approximately ninety percent of Tesla’s current revenue streams. In short, the market is ready to pay extra today based upon the assumption that the existing automotive business model is merely temporary, and the future business model represented by robot taxis and humanoid robots is nearly science fiction. My perspective is just the opposite: when the present day begins to show signs of weakness. Paying multiples of perfection for a speculative future may result in the fastest possible path toward financial pain.

The problem isn't that Tesla is expensive.

What I worry the most about people buying the stock now is one thing: the valuation isn't "off the charts" as a result of an increase in the acceleration of the business, rather, it's as a direct result of the business having given increasingly weak signals in recent quarters. As you can clearly see from the free cash flow chart, the free cash flow has continued to decline at an alarming rate. Free cash flow was around $3.99 Billion in Q3 '25, then fell to just above $1.42 Billion in Q4 '25.

And the company will spend upwards of $20 Billion on capital expenditures alone next year. To put it simply, they are spending such large amounts of money while their ability to convert our growth into dollars continues to decrease. That's the issue. This is absurd!

As long as a company has a growth story, it can afford lower earnings for a while, but when both margins and cash flows begin to contract, the markets should grow more severe, not more lenient.

Profitability erosion graph tells an even clearer story. The operating margin peaked at 10.8% in Q3 2024 before falling all the way to 2.1% in Q1 2025 and then only recovering to the 5.7% range by the end of 2025. The net margin followed almost exactly the same path: From 8.6% to 3.4%.

Finally, there’s the earnings per share chart — and Tesla has missed expectations in five out of the past seven quarters. The most recent “beat” doesn’t change the picture — it appears far more related to short-term positive factors than any material improvement in the fundamentals of the business.

Therefore, it’s not simply that Tesla is expensive. The issue is that Tesla is valued like a company that executed perfectly well, while the numbers (quarter after quarter) tell a story which is quite different. This represents (in my view), where people who buy the stock today make their biggest mistake -- they are willing to pay top dollar at the worst possible moment given that the underlying quality of the business no longer supports such valuations.

The risks of my thesis

I could be incorrect about the narrative associated with the stock, as opposed to the valuation itself. Many investors do not consider valuation when investing in companies they believe have good vision and management. Therefore, the value of the stock may increase regardless of whether or not the valuation is extremely high and disproportionate relative to a comparable stock such as Tesla.

Also, we should take into account that according to Reuters (as I said before) there was a significant resurgence in European automobile registrations. In France, Norwegian, Swedish, Danish and other European countries, Tesla registration has surged. Additionally, German Tesla registrations were more than four times higher during 2025 compared to 2024. Additionally, lower priced versions of the Model 3 and Model Y could provide volume growth and also provide better utilization of plants. From a software standpoint, by the end of Q4 2025, Tesla reported that active FSD subscription levels rose to 1.1M and stated that they expect monthly subscription rates to grow over two times in 2025. In terms of operations, the robotaxi service launched in Austin is gaining momentum. Additionally, Tesla has started to remove the safety monitor from a limited number of rides. Lastly, at the close of 2025, Tesla held approximately $44.1B in cash and/or equivalents and/or investments.

My position remains the same: although these are examples of why I believe the Company has alternatives available to it, they are not sufficient reasons for me to conclude that the current price represents a bargain. In terms of energy alone, Tesla deployed 46.7 GWh of energy in 2025 and grew its segment revenue by 27% year/year. As well, Tesla continues to invest in both computing and production equipment.

And finally, Tesla does have an AI alternative. The issue here is simply the price at which that alternative is already included in the multiple at which I am purchasing. It is insufficient for me to believe that the story behind the Company is believable if I am paying extreme multiples for the stock. For me to purchase a stock at extreme multiples, I require that it significantly outperform expectations. At present, the trend in Tesla's financial results are moving in the opposite direction.

Conclusion

While Tesla will continue to be an innovative icon that can change whole markets as it has done many times before; A good business does not necessarily translate into a good investment. Today we have a stock trading at extremely high multiples, a car business that is showing signs of slowing down, deliveries were only 358,000 versus production capacity of 408,000 units, capex for 2026 is projected to exceed 20 Billion dollars, and the full potential of its artificial intelligence initiatives are yet to be fully realized. As such, I am once again stating my position: Strong Sell. It's not that I dislike the company. Rather, it is based on the principles of discipline. Discipline tells me to avoid this one.

data.title: AT&T Before The Bell: 5 Key Metrics To Watch On April 22nd
data.description:
Introduction

AT&T Inc. (T) has been on a wild ride since I last gave it a Buy rating, as it jumped over $28 per share but has since declined to reach around my published price. With the company’s earnings incoming, I want to dive into what I’m looking forward to the most.

What Has Changed Since My Last Analysis

My previous article was written when AT&T was still in the process of completing two transformative deals. Both have now either closed or are delivering results ahead of schedule. When I last wrote about AT&T, it was still going through two transformative deals, but it has advanced on these dynamics. The deal for Lumen Technologies, Inc.'s Mass Markets (LUMN) officially closed earlier in February for $5.75B and will add over 1MM fiber subscribers across more than 4MM locations in major cities like Denver and Seattle. This will help T in its pursuit of reaching 40MM total fiber passings by the end of the year, with the ultimate goal being 60MM by 2030.

For the EchoStar Corporation (SATS), the deal is not formally closed, but it is already delivering results, as T secured a short-term spectrum manager lease that allows the firm to use the 3.45 GHz midband spectrum ahead of FCC approval. The impact is direct, as T has been reporting download speeds up to 80% faster on mobile and 55% faster for wireless. When the $23B deal closes, which is set for mid-2026, so relatively soon, the company will add around 50 MHz of combined low-band and mid-band spectrum nationwide, effectively closing the gap with T-Mobile.

Five Things I’m Watching On April 22nd

I believe that the single most important dynamic to look at will be the net fiber adds with the Lumen integration. In Q4, T added 283K fiber subscribers organically, but the Q1 numbers will be the first ones to include Lumen’s existing subscriber base. I want to see the organic run rate holding above 250K with management providing guidance on how they can quickly cross-sell wireless into the newly acquired footprint. The opportunity in the new metro areas is simply massive, and if management signals a 50% convergence target in the Lumen markets within 3 years, it would be extremely bullish for AT&T.

On another front, T added 421K postpaid phone subscribers in the latest quarter. This market remains one of the most competitive, and T-Mobile continues to outpace AT&T as well as Verizon on raw subscriber additions with 962K in Q4. I’ll be closely watching postpaid phone ARPU growth and churn instead of the headline number. As the fiber footprint expands, the wireless business should benefit from structural churn improvement even if the headline number remains in the 350K to 450K range.

Q1 will also be the first quarter under the new reporting structure for the company, which has been redesigned to better reflect the fiber and 5G growth story. I will want to see how the firm presents the integration of Lumen and whether these two new segments provide a greater view into fiber economics. This has historically been a point of frustration for investors, as it has been difficult to isolate the high-growth fiber and wireless businesses from the legacy copper and business wireline operations. If this satisfies what we have wanted for a while now, I believe that the firm could see a better valuation multiple on the market.

T has been guiding for $18B+ in free cash flow for the year, implying an FCF yield of over 9%, which is incredible for today’s market. And this goes with the reportedly strong growth posted by the firm, as EBITDA is set to reach a growth rate of 3 to 4% with a clear path for re-acceleration through 2028. Though Q1 is generally weaker than the rest of the year due to seasonality, I still expect the firm to post a $4B FCF level.

On the debt front, the Lumen deal added debt, pushing the net debt to EBITDA level to around 3.2x, though management has stated that they want to return to the 2.5x target range within 3 years. I want to see what the debt balance will look like in the quarter and want to see if management will confirm the deleveraging timeline. I believe that the debt profile has been one of the most overlooked aspects of the turnaround, as it has already come down from historical levels, especially with the TimeWarner disaster.

Valuation

I’ll say that AT&T is cheap on all comparable metrics, as its P/E GAAP stands at 8.35, below Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ) at 11.09 and T-Mobile US, Inc. (TMUS) at a staggering 19.57. On an FCF basis, the yield currently stands at 8.8%, which is one of the lowest levels over the past 10 years, though I expect it to rebound considering the guidance of $18B for 2026. Even on an EV/EBITDA basis with the debt level, AT&T is relatively cheap at 7.62, which is around the middle of its peers.

The biggest growth metric to follow would be the firm’s convergence rate, which is essentially the percentage of customers who subscribe to both fiber and wireless, reaching a staggering 42%. This is highly critical to have a better view of the trajectory ahead, as these converged customers are fundamentally more profitable, as they exhibit significantly lower churn rates and tend to purchase higher speed tiers.

Risks Heading Into Earnings

The biggest systemic risk to AT&T would be its still highly leveraged balance sheet, combined with the sustainably high-interest rate environment. As with the two major acquisitions, the firm’s leverage level is still highly above its 3-year target. The persistent CapEx requirements and the near-debt maturities create a major refinancing risk. Plus, with the current war in the Middle East, there is a risk that the heightened inflation environment will push the Fed to keep higher rates for the time being.

AT&T is also facing major legal and environmental scrutiny over its legacy network of toxic-lead-sheathed copper cables. The company is battling a consolidated class-action lawsuit from public pension funds claiming that T failed to disclose these liabilities to investors.

Conclusion

I believe that the two deals will essentially help AT&T to dominate the connectivity market, especially with the Lumen assets that will expand the physical fixed-line footprint across 11 new states. By also deploying the 50 MHz of Echostar spectrum rapidly, it has solved the mid-band mobile capacity constraints that previously hurt its 5G performance, delivering an 80% speed augmentation that fortifies its wireless product without waiting for federal deal closures. With the incoming earnings, which I believe will be positive, I give AT&T a Buy rating.

data.title: NewAmsterdam Pharma: Cholesterol-Lowering Player Is A Risk-On Buy Today
data.description:
Investment Overview

NewAmsterdam Pharma (NAMS) is a Naarden, Netherlands-based Pharma company that joined the Nasdaq in November 2022 via a business combination with Frazier Lifesciences Acquisition Corporation, a special purpose acquisition company ("SPAC")—according to a press release at the time:

Proceeds from this transaction were approximately $328 million, prior to deducting transaction expenses, comprising approximately $93 million in funds from the former FLAC trust account and approximately $235 million from the concurrent, oversubscribed PIPE financing

The company uses the following business description in its 2025 annual report/10K filing as follows:

We seek to fill a significant unmet need for a safe, well tolerated and convenient low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (“LDL-C”) lowering therapy.

In multiple Phase 3 trials, we have investigated obicetrapib, an oral, low-dose, once-daily, highly selective cholesteryl ester transfer protein (“CETP”) inhibitor, alone or as a fixed-dose combination (“FDC”) with ezetimibe, as preferred LDL-C lowering therapies to be used as an adjunct to statin therapy for patients at risk of cardiovascular disease (“CVD”) with elevated LDL-C, for whom existing therapies are not sufficiently effective or well tolerated.

Additionally, we believe that CETP inhibition may also play a role in other indications by potentially mitigating the risk of developing diseases such as Alzheimer’s disease.

Obicetrabip - Progress To Date

In December 2024, NAMS stock jumped from ~$7 to ~$22 as the company reported data from a Phase 3 study in patients with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (“ASCVD”). The primary endpoint was the least-squares mean of the percent change in LDL-C from baseline to day 84 for obicetrapib 10 mg compared to placebo, and it was met, obicetrapib achieving a mean 35% change versus 2% for placebo. The press release also noted that:

As part of the safety analysis, the trial adjudicated MACE (Major Adverse Cardiovascular Events), including death, non-fatal myocardial infarction, non-fatal stroke and coronary revascularization. In addition, a 21% reduction in MACE favoring obicetrapib was observed.

NewAmsterdam has now completed three Phase 3 studies, and in each, the company says obicetrapib met its primary and secondary endpoints, with statistically significant reductions in LDL-C observed. In terms of unmet need, the Naarden, Netherlands-based biotech notes:

A significant proportion of patients with high cholesterol do not achieve acceptable LDL-C levels using statin therapy alone. We estimate that in the United States there are approximately 30 million patients that are not at their risk-based LDL-C goals despite treatment with lipid lowering therapy, including approximately 13 million with ASCVD.

Since December 2024, NewAmsterdam stock has experienced some ups and downs, but today it trades at ~$34 per share (pre-market), up a further ~35%. Reporting full-year earnings and business updates in February this year, NewAmsterdam notes that:

Marketing Authorization Applications ("MAAs") for obicetrapib and the fixed dose combination were accepted for review by the European Medicines Agency (“EMA”), Switzerland, and United Kingdom regulators, and we anticipate a decision from each in the second half of 2026.

In parallel, together with our partner Menarini, we are actively preparing for a potential commercial launch in Europe. In the United States, we continue to expand our commercial capabilities with the notable hiring of Steve Albers, former senior vice president of market access and public affairs at Novo Nordisk, who will now lead our market access and public affairs efforts, further strengthening our established team.”

The MAAs were accepted for patients with primary hypercholesterolemia, both heterozygous familial (“HeFH”) and non-familial or mixed dyslipidemia. Its partner in the EU is Menarini, who will market and sell the drug, if approved, with NewAmsterdam receiving tiered double-digit percentage royalties ranging from the low double-digits to mid-twenties on net sales, and up to an additional €833 million upon the achievement of various clinical, regulatory, and commercial milestones.

Meanwhile, more studies are being run, as shown below (source: corporate presentation):

The drug has established a potentially competitive profile, as we can see below:

NewAmsterdam ultimately wants to secure approval for obicetrapib as a monotherapy, and in combo with ezetimibe—marketed and sold as Zetia by Organon (OGN)—based on results from its TANDEM study, which showed a mean LDL-C reduction of 52% in patients treated with a combination of 10 mg of obicetrapib and 10 mg of ezetimibe as an adjunct to high-intensity statins when compared to placebo.

Seemingly, before filing an NDA in the US, the company may wait for final data from its PREVAIL study, a cardiovascular outcomes ("CVOT") study that is expected to read out final data this year, although management told analysts at a recent healthcare conference:

when we surveyed physicians, 90%, the outcome study is not critical for the decision to prescribe obicetrapib, about 10% it is. And so we want to make sure that when we launch, we have maximized the label. And so we believe that having a launch that has the outcome study either in the label or soon after that with an updated label is going to be important.

We also have the RUBENS study, which is going to finish at the end of this year -- sorry, the end of '26 that we'd like to get in the label as well, which is the diabetes trial.

There are studies in Alzheimer's and studies of obicetrapib in combo with a PCSK9 inhibitor such as Amgen's (AMGN) Repatha also ongoing, and as of the end of 2025, NewAmsterdam reported a cash position of $729m, against a net loss for the year of $(203.8m); therefore, funding would appear to be sufficient for 3-4 more years at the current rate of burn, although financing a regulatory submission, and if successful, a commercial launch, would be a significant drain on resources.

Analysis - Assessing The Investment Opportunity In Play Today

In summary, NewAmsterdam appears to have an intriguing drug on its hands—an orally available complement to statins that has met multiple pivotal study endpoints and appears to be a significant improvement on prior CETP inhibitors, as shown in the slide below:

Some research online tells me that Pfizer (PFE) discontinued studies of torcetrapib on safety grounds, Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) discontinued development due to lack of efficacy, and Eli Lilly (LLY) discontinued development of evacetrapib despite some promising mid-stage data, again on efficacy grounds. Merck also abandoned development of anacetrapib in 2017.

If four of the world's largest Pharmas cannot make a CETP inhibitor work, should we therefore be concerned for obicetrapib, especially given NAMS has no other clinical stage assets of note?

Ultimately, as noted in the slide above, and also in NAMS' 10K filing, the BROADWAY and BROOKLYN studies met their primary efficacy endpoint, with " safety and tolerability comparable to placebo," and there were ~2,500 patients in the former study, which does seem to make a compelling case for approval.

Cautious investors may prefer to wait and see the results of the PREVAIL study, however—according to NAMS:

In March 2022, we commenced our Phase 3 PREVAIL CVOT, which is designed to assess the potential of obicetrapib to reduce occurrences of MACE, including cardiovascular death, non-fatal myocardial infarction, non-fatal stroke and non-elective coronary revascularization in at least 9,000 patients.

We completed enrollment in PREVAIL in April 2024. PREVAIL will continue until the last participant has been followed for a minimum of 2.5 years and the target number of MACE events have occurred.

As a result, the earliest the trial could conclude based on the minimum follow-up period is the end of 2026. However, we will continue the trial until the target number of MACE events occur, which could likely require the trial to continue beyond this point.

Meanwhile, approvals in Europe this year could open up a nice royalty stream for NewAmsterdam, even if the "holy grail" is likely approval in the US. In that sense, the variety of studies being conducted could either be a help or a hindrance in my view—on one hand, it showcases the "all court" nature of the drug; on the other, does obicetrapib score highly enough in any category to warrant approval in a crowded space?

NAMS' competitors are highlighted in its latest 10K filing as follows:

If approved, obicetrapib would compete with approved non-statin treatments such as ezetimibe, Nexletol/Nexlizet and PCSK9 inhibitors such as Repatha, Praluent and Leqvio.

We are also aware of two orally administered small molecule product candidates that target the PCSK9 protein as a mechanism to lower LDL-C and reduce the risk of ASCVD in various stages of clinical development. These consist of MK-0616 (Enlicitide) from Merck & Co (MRK) for which Merck released data from completed Phase 3 trials of adult patients with hypercholesterolemia in November 2025 and, if approved, has the potential to enter the U.S. market in 2026, and AZD0780 from AstraZeneca, which is being evaluated in an ongoing Phase 3 clinical trial.

There are also a number of other product candidates in clinical development by third parties, such as Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals (ARWR), CVI Pharmaceuticals, Innovent Biologics, Ionis Pharmaceuticals (IONS), Lib Therapeutics, Novartis (NVS), Novo Nordisk (NVO), Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (REGN), Verve Therapeutics (now part of Eli Lilly) and others, that are intended to treat ASCVD by lowering LDL-C and/or Lp(a).

Repatha earned ~$3bn in revenues in 2025, and Leqvio ~$1.2bn, underscoring the potential opportunity ahead for NewAmsterdam, whose market cap is presently $3.9bn.

I think that valuation reflects some uncertainty on Wall Street, and there is significant single-asset risk here; however, the overall body of data collected to date supports a positive outlook, I'd say, and Alzheimer's is an intriguing potential catalyst—according to the company's 2025 earnings press release:

In ApoE4/E4 carriers, the highest risk category for Alzheimer’s disease, obicetrapib was observed to reduce p-tau217 levels by 20.5%, over 12 months, compared to placebo (p=0.010, n=29).

As the CEO put it during his recent fireside chat:

So it's all about that journey and the patients wanting to deal with these very personal things for them. The Lp(a) have a very bad family history of heart disease. In diabetes, of course, they worry about all the other comorbidities. And for APOE e4, nothing is more motivating than knowing that they don't start something soon. They're destined to get Alzheimer's dementia. So to me, it's going to be a rocket for obicetrapib for those reasons.

A drug with a three-pronged benefit in cholesterol, diabetes, and Alzheimer's certainly has significant promise in commercial markets, in my view, and I wonder if approvals in the EU will be the catalysts for a long and fruitful era as a commercial pharma for NewAmsterdam, or, failing that, a buyout by a large Pharma concern.

I'd recommend watching this space and especially watching out for the RUBENS and PREVAIL study data due this year, which I'd speculate has the potential to double or halve NAMS' share price, depending on its success. Personally, I favour the optimistic scenario, but a risk-averse investor may prefer to play a wait-and-see game.

data.title: Lord Abbett Short Duration Income Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

  • The first quarter was defined by a balance between economic resilience and rising geopolitical and macro uncertainty. Market performance faced headwinds from weakness in Technology/Software (driven by AI disruption concerns) and escalating geopolitical tensions, though supportive macroeconomic conditions helped offset some of the pressure. Labor market signals were mixed and inflation remained above target, but expectations for earnings growth, fiscal stimulus, and eventual tariff stabilization helped anchor the outlook. Late in the quarter, geopolitical risks intensified as tensions in the Middle East disrupted energy markets and pushed oil prices higher, delaying expectations for rate cuts and introducing near-term inflation risks. Despite this backdrop, the underlying economy remained constructive, supported by solid consumer spending, continued earnings momentum, and expectations for longer-term policy support.
  • Over the quarter, short-term investment grade corporate 2 credit spreads widened by 11 basis points (bps), short-term commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) 3 spreads tightened by 4 bps, and short-term asset backed securities (ABS) 4 spreads were flat. The 2-Year U.S. Treasury yield increased from 3.48% to 3.79% and the 10-Year U.S. Treasury yield moved higher from 4.17% to 4.32% 1 .

Portfolio Review

  • The Fund returned 0.20%, reflecting performance at the net asset value (NAV) of Class I shares with all distributions reinvested, for the quarter ended March 31st , 2026. The Fund's benchmark, the ICE BofA 1-3 Year U.S. Corporate Index 5 returned 0.33% during the same period.
  • The portfolio's rates positioning was the primary detractor from relative performance over the quarter. In particular, the portfolio's modestly longer duration detracted from relative performance as short-term rates moved higher over the quarter. While the portfolio's duration positioning is longer than the benchmark, it is more in line with its peer group.
  • Security selection within investment grade corporate bonds also detracted from relative performance, mainly within the Financial and Technology sectors. Over the quarter, we increased the portfolio's allocation to investment grade corporate bonds, particularly within the Banking sector. We felt that larger banks became more attractive during the volatility in the back half of the quarter and align with our preference for sectors less exposed to AI-driven disruption.
  • The portfolio's allocation to CMBS was the primary contributor to relative performance. Over the quarter, we modestly increased allocations to CMBS, particularly AAA-rated conduit deals, to take advantage of attractive relative value.
  • The portfolio's allocation to asset-backed securities (ABS) also contributed to relative performance. Over the quarter, we modestly decreased the portfolio's allocation to ABS and used the proceeds to fund purchases in corporate credit to take advantage of widening spreads.

Outlook

  • At the close of the first quarter, U.S. Treasury yields across the curve are near their highest levels since the summer of 2025 and remain elevated relative to most of the past decade. This elevated starting point enhances expected return potential and improves the risk/reward trade-off, as higher yields can help mitigate the impact of further rate volatility.
  • There are two primary forces driving markets as we move beyond the first quarter. Disruption risk from artificial intelligence continues to emerge as a longer-term force, with markets increasingly pricing in its potential impact on certain industries and future earnings. The U.S.-Iran conflict is adding to market volatility through elevated oil prices, renewed inflation pressures, and uncertainties around growth. While fundamentals and the macro backdrop were relatively supportive coming into 2026, elevated geopolitical risk, AI-related uncertainty, and tight spreads keep us cautious in the near term.
  • In response to the evolving impact of AI, we have been repositioning the portfolio away from areas most exposed to its first-order effects, especially software. This has included reducing select technology exposures, as well as positions in business development companies and insurance issuers tied to private credit along with decreasing CLOs. With the escalation of the Iran conflict, we've kept overall risk exposure closer to the peer group and remain cautious on consumer cyclicals until we have better visibility over the medium-term outlook.
  • The portfolio maintains a relatively conservative, balanced posture and ample liquidity. Within corporates, we are conservatively positioned, favoring industries focused on physical assets, such as utilities, energy, healthcare and industrials, along with larger money center and investment banks. In the securitized portion of the portfolio, we favor highly liquid, AAA-rated conduit CMBS diversified across real estate assets.

In general, we believe markets like these offer great opportunities for active managers. Given our multi-sector approach, there are multiple levers that can be employed to potentially generate excess returns (including optimal sector/industry/rating/security positioning, duration, and yield curve positioning, etc.) in this environment.

data.title: Artisan Sustainable Emerging Markets Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Portfolio Discussion

Emerging markets (EM) equities ended Q1 lower, as strong initial momentum reversed in March. The quarter unfolded in two distinct phases. Early in the period, improving investor sentiment toward lower global rates supported a constructive backdrop for EM. However, conditions deteriorated as geopolitical tensions—particularly the conflict in the Middle East—triggered an oil price shock and a broad-based risk-off environment. Korea and Taiwan were the MSCI Emerging Markets Index's top contributors, while China and India were its largest detractors. Our portfolio performed in line with the benchmark in Q1.

The main relative contributors in Q1 included SK Hynix, Vista Energy (VIST) and Contemporary Amperex Technology Co Ltd (CATL). SK Hynix is a leading Korean semiconductor manufacturer. Continued strength in memory chip demand, driven by chip use in artificial intelligence (AI) applications, lifted shares of SK Hynix. The rapid scaling of AI workloads has driven increased demand for high-bandwidth memory, which is critical for processing and transferring large volumes of data. Equity market gains extended across the broader memory industry, with Samsung Electronics also ending higher. While we believe both companies are positioned to benefit from sustained AI-related demand, we do not hold Samsung in the portfolio. In our view, SK Hynix's technological leadership supports a superior competitive position in this space. At the same time, we remain mindful that the pace and magnitude of recent dynamic random-access memory (DRAM) price increases may not be sustainable, as elevated costs could begin to weigh on end demand. While we maintain our conviction in SK Hynix, we continue to monitor these dynamics closely.

Vista Energy is an Argentine oil and gas company. Shares advanced as investors responded to improving prospects for Argentina's energy sector. The company recently announced a strategic acquisition of shale assets in the Vaca Muerta formation, expanding its production capacity and future drilling opportunities. Vista has operated through a historically difficult regulatory environment in Argentina, characterized by policy volatility, capital controls and constraints on energy pricing and exports. Despite these challenges, the company has executed consistently, demonstrating operational discipline and an ability to navigate complex conditions. We believe Vista is well positioned to play a leading role in Argentina's growing energy export market, supported by its strong asset base and proven track record.

CATL is a leading Chinese manufacturer of lithium-ion batteries used in electric vehicles and energy storage systems. The company continues to demonstrate its technological leadership, including progress in next-generation sodium-ion batteries, with early commercialization underway. More recently, the conflict in the Middle East has reinforced the importance of energy security, further accelerating investment in electrification and clean energy solutions. This dynamic is also increasing the need for energy storage and grid stability, where batteries play a critical role in enabling the integration of renewable power. We believe CATL is well positioned to benefit from these trends, given its scale, innovation and leading role in the global battery market.

The main relative detractors in Q1 included E Ink, Trip.com (TCOM) and MercadoLibre (MELI). E Ink is a Taiwan-based producer of e-paper technology used in e-readers, electronic shelf labels (ESL) and digital signage. Shares declined during the quarter, reflecting softer near-term revenue and broader investor preference for technology companies with more direct exposure to AI applications. Despite recent headwinds, we continue to see several drivers of long-term growth. Adoption of ESL systems remains strong, supported by ongoing rollouts among large global retailers and an expanding pipeline of new customers. In addition, demand is increasing for digital signage and color displays in consumer devices. We have long held E Ink in the portfolio and reaffirm our conviction in the company. We believe in the widespread adoption of e-paper technology and recognize the critical role E Ink plays in enabling this transition.

Trip.com is one of China's largest online travel agencies. Shares came under pressure after Chinese regulators opened an antitrust investigation into the company, introducing near-term uncertainty. While details of the investigation remain limited, regulatory scrutiny in China has increasingly focused on pricing practices and competitive behavior, particularly efforts to curb excessive competition within certain industries. In this context, we view the development as part of a broader regulatory framework. Underlying travel demand remains resilient, supported by continued recovery in both domestic and international bookings. As consumer demand strengthens, we believe the company will benefit from its leading platform, extensive supplier relationships and strong brand recognition.

MercadoLibre is the leading e-commerce platform in Latin America. The stock faced pressure from increased competitive intensity in key markets, including initiatives by global players, such as Amazon, to reduce fees in regions like Mexico. Competition in Brazil, in particular, has intensified, prompting MercadoLibre to increase investment to reinforce its market share. We view these dynamics as a natural evolution of a growing market rather than a structural change in the company's competitive position. In our view, MercadoLibre's scale, integrated e-commerce and fintech platform, and logistics capabilities provide durable competitive advantages that are difficult to replicate. We remain constructive on the company's long-term growth opportunity as it continues to deepen engagement across commerce and financial services.

Portfolio Activity

During the quarter, we took advantage of market volatility to add several new names to the portfolio. We established a position in CIMC Enric, a Chinese industrial and clean energy solutions provider. The company is investing in new areas of growth, including waste-gas-to-fuel projects that convert industrial byproducts into liquefied natural gas and hydrogen, as well as emerging opportunities in green methanol. In our view, these initiatives, combined with the company's integrated business model and established track record, reflect a shift beyond equipment manufacturing toward a more integrated role in clean fuel production.

We also added Kazatomprom, the world's largest uranium producer and a key supplier to the global nuclear energy industry. Increasing demand for reliable, low-carbon baseload power is supporting renewed interest in nuclear energy as countries seek to balance decarbonization goals with energy security. We view Kazatomprom's low-cost asset base and strategic position within the nuclear fuel supply chain as key competitive advantages in an increasingly important part of the global energy system.

Within financials, we purchased Banca Transilvania and Nu Holdings (Nubank) (NU). Banca Transilvania is Romania's largest bank. The company has expanded its footprint through a combination of organic growth and acquisitions, strengthening its presence across regions and customer segments. Romania remains one of the most underbanked countries in Europe, especially across retail and small- and medium-sized enterprise (SME) segments, which we believe creates a long runway for client growth.

Nubank is a Brazil-based digital bank with a differentiated model supported by its scale, low customer acquisition costs and a proprietary technology platform. The company offers a broad range of services, including banking, lending and payments, to a rapidly expanding customer base, enabling deeper engagement across its ecosystem. It has also demonstrated the ability to navigate periods of macroeconomic volatility by maintaining a conservative lending approach, particularly across lower income customer segments. We believe this combination of engagement and disciplined underwriting supports durable growth over time.

Perspective

Looking ahead to the rest of the year, the near-term outlook for EM remains uncertain. The recent oil shock may delay the anticipated path of interest rate declines across many EM economies, and inflation could reaccelerate in certain regions. The geopolitical backdrop has also become more challenging. We entered the year hoping for progress toward ending the war in Ukraine and are now facing a second conflict in the Middle East, both with real consequences for the people across EM.

We are encouraged by the resilience we've seen at the company level, supported by adaptable business models, strong balance sheets and disciplined capital allocation. Through extensive travel and on-the-ground engagement during the quarter, we have observed how management teams are navigating this environment and positioning their businesses for long-term growth. Market volatility can lead to indiscriminate selloffs, but it also creates opportunities to invest in high-quality businesses at more attractive valuations. We have used the recent dislocation to invest in companies with sustainable competitive advantages and unique access to growth—businesses that we believe are capable of weathering periods of stress.

While volatility remains elevated, our conviction in the long-term EM opportunity set is unchanged. We remain focused on identifying companies with strong management teams and the ability to navigate complex operating environments. As always, our approach is grounded in disciplined, bottom-up stock selection, which we believe is the most effective way to navigate inherent EM volatility.

data.title: AUD/USD 360 Pips Rally At Risk Of A Minor Mean Reversion Decline Below 0.7200 Before New Upleg
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

The Australian dollar has benefited significantly since last Wednesday, April 8, when the US and Iran agreed to a ceasefire due to its higher beta factor, as the AUD has mirrored the movement of risk assets such as equities since the start of the US-Iran war on February 28, 2026, exhibiting similar risk-off movements, ignoring the hawkish monetary policy guidance advocated by the RBA.

The AUD/USD has jumped by 230 pips (+3.3%) from the April 8, 2026 low to print an intraday high of 0.7198 on Thursday, April 16, 2026 at this time of writing, just a whisker above its prior March 11, 2026 high of 0.7188.

When measured from its current minor uptrend low of 0.6833 printed on March 30, 2026, it has rallied by almost 360 pips (+5.3%), making the Australian dollar the second-best performing major currency against the US dollar based on a one-month rolling performance; USD/AUD (-2.47%), just behind the euro, where the USD/EUR slid -3.04% (see Fig. 1).

However, price actions of highly liquid tradable instruments do move vertically but oscillate within trends.

Technically speaking, the three-week bullish impulsive up move on the AUD/USD from its March 30, 2026 low of 0.6833 is now due for a minor mean reversion corrective decline.

Let’s now focus on the technical factors to determine AUD/USD’s potential short-term trajectory (1-3 days).

AUD/USD - Potential minor corrective decline towards 50-day moving average

Watch the 0.7188/0.7200 key short-term pivotal resistance on the AUD/USD. A break below 0.7120 increases the odds of a minor mean reversion decline towards the next intermediate supports of 0.7080 and 0.7033 (also the 50-day moving average) before the next bullish impulsive up-move sequence materializes (see Fig. 2).

On the flip side, a clearance and an hourly close above 0.7200 invalidates the bearish bias for a continuation of the bullish move towards 0.7244/0.7265 and 0.7300 in the first step.

Key elements to support the near-term bearish bias on the AUD/USD

  • The hourly RSI momentum indicator has exited from its overbought region and broken a key ascending support from April 13, 2026.
  • Today’s current intraday high of 0.7198 has hit the upper boundary of the minor ascending channel in place since the April 2, 2026 low.

data.title: Artisan International Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Investing Environment

Global markets delivered mixed results in Q1 2026, with international equities generally outperforming the US for much of the period. Early optimism was supported by easing inflation in parts of Europe and growing confidence that monetary policy would become more accommodative later in the year. Asian emerging markets were a notable area of strength, led by technology hardware companies benefiting from sustained demand tied to artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure investment. Japan also saw support from improving inflation trends and more constructive currency dynamics, while leadership in the US broadened beyond mega-cap technology into more defensive and value-oriented areas of the market.

As the quarter progressed, investor sentiment became more uneven. AI remained an important market driver, particularly across semiconductors, hardware and data center-related infrastructure. New AI agents released by Anthropic intensified investor concern that AI adoption could threaten software companies, leading to an industry selloff. At the same time, parts of the global market faced pressure from shifting economic growth and inflation expectations, as well as concerns that recent gains may not hold up, particularly in sectors that had benefited most from enthusiasm around new AI developments. Beyond AI, broader market conditions were increasingly shaped by policy uncertainty and geopolitical developments. Trade tensions reemerged, and concerns around tariffs added to investor caution.

Geopolitical risk became the dominant market influence by quarter-end. Escalating conflict in the Middle East and the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz drove energy prices sharply higher, weighing on markets globally, particularly in Europe, where rising energy costs pushed inflation further above the European Central Bank's target. Asia also experienced increased volatility due to its reliance on Middle Eastern oil, although a late-quarter rebound in the information technology (IT) sector supported regional performance. Overall, the quarter ended with a more volatile backdrop, as resilient demand tied to AI and other structural growth areas was increasingly offset by inflationary pressure, energy market disruption and heightened geopolitical uncertainty.

Portfolio Performance

In Q1, the portfolio outperformed the MSCI EAFE and MSCI All Country World ex US indices, as both benchmarks posted negative absolute returns.

Both the electrification and defense and aerospace investment themes produced strong results. Stock selection drove performance, particularly in the industrials and IT sectors. A limited exposure to consumer discretionary and an overweight position in utilities provided additional support. The portfolio's lack of exposure to the energy sector hurt relative performance as the sector produced healthy gains in the wake of the Iranian conflict. Regionally, emerging markets holdings fared the best, followed by Japanese holdings.

Starting with our electrification theme, several holdings contributed positively during the quarter as investors continued to reward companies exposed to grid expansion, transmission investment and rising electricity demand, particularly those tied to AI infrastructure. LS Electric was among the strongest performers. The company reported healthy earnings for 2025, with new orders growing 30% year over year and EPS close behind at 28% year over year. Investor sentiment strengthened further based on the company's growing exposure to North American AI data center power infrastructure and constrained global transformer supply. Model usage reports from large AI companies reinforced astronomical energy consumption projections for the space, supporting the market's view that the company is well positioned to benefit from rising power equipment demand. National Grid also contributed positively during the quarter. In March, the company extended and upgraded its five-year financial framework through 2031; it raised its capital investment target to at least £70 billion, asset growth to around 10% and compounded underlying EPS growth to 8% to 10% from its FY26 baseline. We believe the combination of the upgraded framework, stressed electricity supply chains and continued progress under UK's regulatory framework reinforces National Grid's appeal as a regulated utility with visible earnings growth tied to transmission modernization in both the UK and the US.

Conversely, SPIE (SPIWF) was a detractor during the quarter. Its shares traded lower due to near-term concerns about higher energy costs and weaker-than-expected organic growth for 2025 in several regions, and a full-year 2025 free cash flow total that fell short of its target. However, we believe the market reaction was overdone, and investors failed to recognize the durability of SPIE's long-term growth opportunities. Management also raised its medium-term EBITDA margin target, reflecting confidence in continued margin improvement driven by operating leverage and acquisition integration as well as growing demand across transmission and distribution projects, data center buildouts and industrial automation.

In the defense and aerospace theme, geopolitical turmoil drove up shares of our Korean defense holdings. While individual conflicts may be episodic, they reflect a broader rise in global tensions, supporting sustained growth in defense spending. LIG Nex1 and Hanwha Aerospace were among the portfolio's strongest contributors during the quarter, reflecting continued investor enthusiasm toward South Korean defense companies as their global relevance has been increasing.

Investors focused on LIG Nex1's air defense exposure. Management highlighted a growing overseas backlog, ongoing manufacturing expansion and continued strong export demand for its medium-range surface-to-air missile (M-SAM) systems that can intercept ballistic missiles. An important caveat to that point—overseas orders for Korean defense equipment carry higher margins than domestic business. The company's shorter lead times relative to many European and US industry peers have also become an important advantage in the current environment. More broadly, we believe LIG Nex1 remains attractively positioned through its exposure to missiles, radar, anti-drone systems and other higher value defense electronics, which continue to see rising demand as countries invest in more modern and responsive defense capabilities.

Investor sentiment toward Hanwha Aerospace improved as management highlighted a robust 2026 outlook supported by deliveries to Poland, Egypt and Australia, alongside a growing opportunity set in Saudi Arabia and other European markets. The company ended 2025 with an order backlog of 37.2 trillion won (approximately $25 billion) for land-based systems, providing a window into future potential growth. Shares were additionally supported by Hanwha's acquisition of a stake in Korea Aerospace Industries, which reinforced the company's position in the broader Korean aerospace and defense ecosystem.

Within financials, performance was mixed during the quarter, and the sector posed a headwind to relative returns. UBS (UBS) was one of the main detractors during the quarter. Although the company reported broadly solid Q4 results, the shares traded lower as investors remained focused on uncertainty surrounding potential Swiss capital reforms that could require the bank to hold meaningfully higher levels of capital. Weaker-than-expected net new asset trends in the US wealth management business added to the pressure, as restructuring-related advisor departures continued to weigh on flows. In our view, the market reaction reflected concern that regulatory uncertainty could stunt otherwise solid operating progress, including profitability, ongoing cost savings and continued capital return. In our view, the market overreacted to short-term headwinds, but we will continue to watch developments in the government's decision-making on capital reforms.

BFF Bank (BFFBF), an Italian financial institution focused on factoring and specialty finance, was the portfolio's largest detractor during the quarter. The shares came under pressure after the financial institution was forced to announce €95 million in one-off provisions to de-risk its Italian factoring portfolio. BFF has an excess of invoices owed by Italian public entities, and court rulings have made payment delays more costly than the bank previously expected. The provisions led the bank to lower its 2026 earnings target, and an untimely CEO departure added to investor concerns. While these developments hurt sentiment in the near term and led us to trim the position, we will monitor the situation through the lens of the company's longer-term earnings power.

Portfolio Activity

Activity during the quarter was centered on adding to areas where we see durable long-term demand while also taking advantage of stock-specific opportunities created by market volatility. Our purchases reflected continued conviction in several of the portfolio's core themes, particularly defense and electrification, but we also added in areas where we believe the market is underappreciating earnings durability, asset quality and future growth potential.

One holding we are excited about is CSG, a European defense company that went public in January. We believe the company offers exposure to continued growth in European defense spending, particularly in ammunition, as governments across the region prioritize defense readiness and increase domestic industrial capacity to solidify supply chains. The shares fell in March due to an investigation tied to financial irregularities in its FMG division's 120mm tank ammunition contracts. However, we viewed that weakness as an opportunity to begin building a position in a company operating in an area of sustained demand.

Elsewhere, we added Fujikura (FKURF), a Japanese manufacturer of fiber-optic cables, connectors and wiring solutions used in telecom networks and data centers. We believe the company is well positioned to benefit from rising demand for high-speed connectivity in AI infrastructure, particularly as data centers become more complex and require more sophisticated optical cabling. What attracted us most was Fujikura's operation in a severely supply-constrained market, where pricing is rising in tandem with production growth.

In real estate, we bought into Sun Hung Kai Properties (SUHJF), a Hong Kong realty company that operates and manages hotels, properties, car parking and transportation infrastructure. The company holds a large market share of Hong Kong private real estate, a sector that has been revived by more favorable mortgage rates and a return of mainland China buyers. In its interim results for the first half of FY2026, the company reported a 16.7% increase in underlying profit.

We trimmed our insurance portfolio weighting over the quarter by more than half. We exited positions in Prudential (PRU) and Allianz (ALIZF) after strong share-price performance and solid operating execution made the risk-reward less compelling relative to other opportunities in the portfolio. Prudential reported double-digit growth across several key metrics in 2025 and increased shareholder returns. Allianz delivered a record 2025 operating profit, raised its dividend by 11% and announced a new €2.5 billion share buyback program. We continue to view both companies as high-quality franchises.

We also exited RELX (RELX), a global information and analytics company serving customers across risk, legal, scientific and exhibitions markets. We continue to view RELX as a high-quality information and analytics business with durable competitive advantages. However, Anthropic's announcement that Claude Cowork, its agentic assistant, includes plug-ins for business functions such as legal, finance and sales poses too large a risk of disintermediation to information service companies. We exited our position in light of AI's potential to reshape information and software spaces.

Outlook

Looking ahead, we continue to believe the portfolio is well positioned around several long-duration themes that are increasingly supported by visible capital spending and changing government priorities. Defense remains a key area of conviction, as elevated geopolitical tensions continue to drive greater urgency around procurement, localization and military readiness across Europe, Asia and the Middle East. At the same time, electrification remains one of the most compelling opportunities in the portfolio, supported by rising electricity demand, grid modernization and the growing power needs tied to AI infrastructure.

More broadly, we believe international markets continue to offer an attractive opportunity set relative to the US, particularly in areas where valuations remain more reasonable and earnings growth is supported by structural investment rather than multiple expansion alone. After the MSCI All Country World ex USA Index gained 32% in 2025, compared to an 18% gain for the S&P 500® Index, relative strength carried into early 2026. Broad non-US equities were up roughly 6% in Q1, while the S&P 500® Index was roughly flat over the same period.

While macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty remains elevated, we continue to focus on businesses with strong competitive positions, resilient earnings potential and exposure to long-term spending priorities laid out by governments around the globe. We believe this approach should continue to serve the portfolio well as we move through 2026.
